coredata.dc:title: Low-energy consumption rapid synthesis of high-fluorescence nitrogen-doped carbon dots at room temperature using a combusted shrimp shell combined solution and application to catechins detection
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Yi Chen
Wu, Jun Yi
coredata.dc:description: Shrimp shells are used as the carbon and nitrogen source to produce high-fluorescence nitrogen-carbon dots at room temperature by employing a cutting process based on NaCl crystals without added nitrogen-doped agents. The synthesis process is rapid (<5 min) and easy. The synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) are characterized using photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), scan electron microscopy (SEM), Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, and energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis. The synthesized NCDs exhibit high fluorescence and are effectively used in the instant and fast detection of tea freshness.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Green method
Rapid method
High fluorescence nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Sea food waste
Catechins

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Proposed synthesis method: (a) preparation of the carbon and nitrogen source and production of NCDs at room temperature; (b) UV light irradiation and measurement directions.
PL excitation and emission spectra of the synthesized NCDs.
Fluorescence spectra of the NCDs produced by (a) adding spoiled shrimps; (b) catechins detection in real tea; (c) catechins detection.
TEM images of carbon quantum dots; (a) 70,000 × magnification; (b) 100,000 × magnification.
(a) SEM images and (b) EDX analysis of the synthesized NCDs.
IR spectrum of the synthesized NCDs.

head.ce:title: Low-energy consumption rapid synthesis of high-fluorescence nitrogen-doped carbon dots at room temperature using a combusted shrimp shell combined solution and application to catechins detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Food Science, National Taiwan Ocean University, Keelung 202301, ROC, Taiwan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Food Science, National Taiwan Ocean University, Keelung 202301, ROC, Taiwan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Shrimp shells are used as the carbon and nitrogen source to produce high-fluorescence nitrogen-carbon dots at room temperature by employing a cutting process based on NaCl crystals without added nitrogen-doped agents. The synthesis process is rapid (<5 min) and easy. The synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) are characterized using photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), scan electron microscopy (SEM), Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, and energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis. The synthesized NCDs exhibit high fluorescence and are effectively used in the instant and fast detection of tea freshness.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) exhibit low toxicity and good biocompatibility and can be used in the detection of harmful substances in food [1] and polyphenols [2]. Two main methods are used in the preparation of CDs: the top-down method and the bottom-up method. However, CDs prepared using these two methods exhibit several disadvantages; additionally, the materials used are toxic and the preparation process requires a long time. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of CDs is too low. Adding nitrogen to CDs, resulting in nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs), can enhance their fluorescence intensity [3]. It has been reported in the literature that tea can improve the luminescence efficiency of nanomaterials. Tea is a beverage beneficial to human health because it contains polyphenols, which exhibit antioxidant [7] and antimicrobial [8] properties. Tea polyphenols account for approximately 30–42 % of the solid dry weight of brewed green tea, among which catechins represent about 70 % of the total polyphenols [10]. As catechins constitute the highest proportion of tea polyphenols, detecting catechin content can be used as an effective indicator for estimating the overall polyphenol content in tea. Tea leaves with a high content of polyphenols indicate tea freshness. Currently, there is no method for quickly preparing quantum dots and applying them to the detection of polyphenols in tea. At present, the analysis of tea polyphenols primarily relies on high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), which, although accurate, is time-consuming, requires complex sample preparation, and is not suitable for rapid or on-site detection. Most high-fluorescence NCDs are prepared using toxic organic solvents [4] and require high amounts of energy conditions [5] as well as additional surface modifiers [6]. In this study, we propose a novel method for the quick preparation of high-fluorescence NCDs. The proposed method has low energy consumption and uses nontoxic materials without added nitrogen-doped agents. NCDs synthesized using our method can be used as sensors for the quick detection of catechins in tea for the first time and effectively contribute to food waste recycling.

We used combusted and carbonized shrimp shells as the carbon and nitrogen source for synthesizing NCDs, as shown in Fig. 1 . Specifically, 0.5 g of the carbon and nitrogen source was added to a 10-mL water solution containing 10 g of NaCl. The mixture was placed in a press breaker operated at 3000 rpm for 4 min. The NaCl crystals cut the bulk carbon and nitrogen via the shear force produced by the press breaker, which helped convert the bulk carbon and nitrogen into NCDs at 1 atm at 25 °C. The synthesized light-yellow solution, which contained NCDs and traced amounts of NaCl, was then filtered using a syringe filter. Fig. 1(a) illustrates the proposed synthesis process. Under daylight exposure, the solution appears light yellow; when irradiated with ultraviolet (UV) light irradiation (λ = 350 nm), the solution continuously emits blue fluorescence until the UV light is turned off. This characteristic makes it an ideal fluorescent sensor material for detecting catechins (purchase from Chang Gung Biotechnology). During the experiment, a digital lux meter will be used to analyze the changes in fluorescence intensity, as shown in Fig. 1(b).

Fig. 2 shows the PL spectra of the synthesized NCDs, which appear yellow under standard lighting and blue under UV light irradiation (λ = 350 nm; Fig. 2, inset) and exhibit strong absorption properties. We observe that the PL emission intensity exhibits a peak at 425 nm and moves toward the visible-light wavelength by exciting the dots at 350 nm. The measured PL intensity of the quantum dots was 1400,000 cps and digital lux meter measured the fluorescence brightness to be 88 lux, indicating high-intensity particles.

Fig. 3 (a) shows the fluorescence emission spectra of the NCDs obtained by adding spoiled shrimp solution and without adding spoiled shrimp solution. The detection process involves immersing decaying shrimp in water, allowing TMA (trimethylamine) to diffuse into the water. The TMA-containing solution is then added to the NCDs solution, and the fluorescence change is observed. We observe that compared with fresh shrimps, the spoiled shrimp solution enhance the fluorescence intensity. According to [9], spoiled shrimps contain trimethylamine (TMA). The ammonia hydrogen in TMA combines with the ammonia in the NCDs and increases the fluorescent intensity. The PL spectra show that the intensity increases from 1400,000 cps to 1700,000 cps, and the fluorescence brightness measured by a digital lux meter was 106 Lux, indicating that spoiled shrimp solution can enhance fluorescence, as shown in Fig. 3(a).

Fig. 3(b) shows the fluorescence emission spectra of the NCDs obtained by adding real tea and without adding real tea. We observe that the real tea decreases the fluorescence intensity from 1400,000 cps to 39,000 cps, and the fluorescence brightness measured by a digital lux meter was 59 Lux. We also observe that by adding pure catechins, the NCD fluorescence ceases and the intensity decreases significantly.

Fig. 3(c) shows the fluorescence emission spectra of the NCDs obtained by adding pure catechins and without adding pure catechins. We observe that the catechins decrease the fluorescence intensity from 1400,000 cps to 28,000 cps. The fluorescence intensity was analyzed using a digital lux meter, revealing a decrease in brightness upon the addition of catechins. Specifically, when 1.5 µg/mL of catechins were added to NCDs, the fluorescence intensity decreased from 88 Lux to 85 Lux, indicating a limit of detection (LOD) of 1.5 µg/mL. As the concentration of catechin increased, the fluorescence intensity showed a more pronounced decline. When the catechin concentration reached 400 mg/mL, the fluorescence brightness significantly dropped to only 9 Lux.

TEM images of the synthesized carbon quantum dots are shown in Fig. 4 (a) and (b) using different magnifications. The small black spots represent spherical NCD particles (sized approximately 1–3 nm), which are well dispersed without aggregation. Thus, the synthesized NCDs exhibit good solubility in water.

Fig. 5 (a) shows the SEM images and EDX analysis of the synthesized NCDs. We observe that the shape of the NCDs is spherical (red circles) with a size of 2–5 nm and a uniform size distribution. The EDX analysis (Fig. 5(b)), which was used to determine the NCD composition and estimate the concentrations of carbon (C), oxygen (O2), and nitrogen (N2) in the synthesized NCDs, showed that these elements were dominant.

Fig. 6 shows the FTIR spectroscopy analysis of N-carbon dot surface functional groups. The characteristic absorption broadband at 3360 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibration of the O–H/N–H functional group on the surface of the carbon quantum dots. The characteristic absorption bands at 2129 cm−1 and 1637 cm−1 can be attributed to the N=C=N and C=O stretching, respectively, indicating that the synthesized NCDs are nitrogen-doped.

This study presents an eco-friendly and efficient approach for synthesizing NCDs with high fluorescence. The method, which avoids the use of toxic chemicals, relies on a cutting process using NaCl crystals and employs shrimp shells as the carbon and nitrogen source. The resulting NCDs demonstrated strong blue fluorescence when exposed to UV light and exhibited excellent photostability. Furthermore, they showed good dispersion in aqueous solutions, making them a promising candidate for rapid detection applications.

Yi Chen Huang: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Software, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Jun Yi Wu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Ministry of Science and Technology (MOST) of Taiwan (MOST 111-2222-E-231-001-).
The authors thank the Electron Microscopy Center of the Institute of Marine Biology at National Taiwan Ocean University for providing technical assistance.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Low-energy-consumption rapid synthesis of carbon dots at room temperature from combusted food waste with versatile analytical applications
Food Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wu, J.Y., & Huang, Y.C. (2024). Low-energy-consumption rapid synthesis of carbon dots at room temperature from combusted food waste with versatile analytical applications. Food Chem., 138908.
